title: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs
authors: Jeffrey I. Cohen, Marc G. Ghany

items.title: Combination Therapy for Viral Infections
items.content: Combination therapy using multiple antiviral agents with different mechanisms of action is often more effective than monotherapy, especially against RNA viruses. This approach helps address the dynamic mixture of drug-resistant and drug-sensitive viral strains.
keyPoints: Antiviral drugs typically inhibit replication but do not eradicate latent infections.
RNA viruses have higher mutation rates leading to common resistance; DNA virus resistance is more problematic in immunocompromised patients.
Combination therapy can be effective against RNA viruses with varied resistance patterns.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · Antiviral Chemotherapy, Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs

items.title: Monitoring and Managing Antiviral Therapy Resistance
items.content: Resistance to antiviral agents is common due to viral mutations. Testing for drug resistance can be crucial in patients who do not respond or whose response diminishes over time.
keyPoints: Antiviral resistance is more prevalent in RNA viruses due to higher mutation rates.
Drug-resistant strains can emerge under the pressure of antiviral drugs, necessitating dynamic monitoring.
Testing involves sequencing viral genes or growing virus with varying drug concentrations; quantitative PCR aids in monitoring response for blood-circulating viruses like CMV, HBV, and HCV.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · Antiviral Chemotherapy, Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs

items.title: Mechanism of Action and Resistance in Herpes Treatment
items.content: Acyclovir is phosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase to its active form, inhibiting viral DNA polymerase. Valacyclovir is a prodrug with better absorption, converting rapidly to acyclovir. Both drugs are less effective against CMV and can encounter resistance due to mutations in HSV or VZV thymidine kinase or DNA polymerase.
keyPoints: Acyclovir phosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase
Inhibits viral DNA polymerase, terminates replication
Resistance from mutations in thymidine kinase or DNA polymerase
Famciclovir and penciclovir also affected by these resistance mechanisms
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ACYCLOVIR, VALACYCLOVIR, FAMCICLOVIR, AND PENCICLOVIR

items.title: Dosing Considerations in Renal Insufficiency
items.content: Acyclovir and valacyclovir are excreted by the kidneys; thus, dosing must be adjusted for renal insufficiency to prevent CNS side effects and crystallization-induced renal issues. Famciclovir also requires dose adjustment due to renal excretion.
keyPoints: Dose reduction necessary in renal insufficiency
CNS side effects more common with higher drug levels in renal impairment
Reversible renal insufficiency from IV acyclovir crystallization risk
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ACYCLOVIR, VALACYCLOVIR, FAMCICLOVIR, AND PENCICLOVIR

items.title: Clinical Use and Timing for Herpes Treatment
items.content: Oral acyclovir is effective if started within 6 days of genital herpes onset. Valacyclovir and famciclovir are preferred for better bioavailability and less frequent dosing. Early treatment (within 1 day) with oral or topical agents reduces symptoms duration in recurrent herpes, while zoster treatment should begin within 3 days.
keyPoints: Oral acyclovir effective if started within 6 days of genital herpes
Valacyclovir preferred for better bioavailability and less frequent dosing
Topical acyclovir reduces shedding by 1-2 days if applied within 1 day of symptom onset in recurrent herpes
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ACYCLOVIR, VALACYCLOVIR, FAMCICLOVIR, AND PENCICLOVIR

items.title: Mechanism of Action: Ganciclovir and Valganciclovir in CMV Infections
items.content: Ganciclovir, a deoxyguanosine analog, is activated by phosphorylation via the UL97 protein kinase in CMV-infected cells. This active form inhibits viral DNA polymerase and prevents guanosine triphosphate incorporation into viral DNA.
keyPoints: Ganciclovir is converted to its active form, ganciclovir triphosphate, by cellular kinases after phosphorylation by UL97 protein kinase in CMV-infected cells.
Inhibits both viral DNA polymerase and guanosine triphosphate incorporation into viral DNA.
Used for treatment and prevention of CMV disease in immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · GANCICLOVIR AND VALGANCICLOVIR

items.title: Clinical Use and Resistance: Ganciclovir and Valganciclovir for CMV Infections
items.content: Valganciclovir, a prodrug of ganciclovir with better oral bioavailability, is used to treat severe CMV infections. It's effective in immunocompromised patients and transplant recipients, either preemptively or prophylactically. Resistance can occur due to mutations in UL97 protein kinase or UL54 viral DNA polymerase.
keyPoints: Valganciclovir has better oral bioavailability than ganciclovir; plasma levels of oral valganciclovir and IV ganciclovir are similar.
Used for treatment and prevention of CMV disease in immunocompromised patients, including transplant recipients.
Resistance due to mutations in UL97 protein kinase or UL54 viral DNA polymerase; resistant virus treated with foscarnet or cidofovir.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · GANCICLOVIR AND VALGANCICLOVIR

items.title: Mechanism of Action: Foscarnet vs. Cidofovir
items.content: Foscarnet directly inhibits herpesvirus DNA polymerases by blocking the pyrophosphate binding site, without requiring phosphorylation. In contrast, cidofovir is a deoxycytidine monophosphate analogue that competes with deoxycytidine triphosphate for incorporation into viral DNA after being phosphorylated to its active diphosphate form.
keyPoints: Foscarnet blocks pyrophosphate binding site in herpesvirus DNA polymerases without needing phosphorylation.
Cidofovir is phosphorylated to compete with deoxycytidine triphosphate for incorporation into viral DNA.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · FOSCARNET | CIDOFOVIR

items.title: Nephrotoxicity Management in Foscarnet and Cidofovir Therapy
items.content: Both foscarnet and cidofovir require dose adjustments for renal insufficiency due to their nephrotoxic potential. Nephrotoxicity is common, affecting up to one-third of patients on foscarnet and about one-fifth on cidofovir. Strategies to mitigate this include administering IV saline before and after each dose for foscarnet and using probenecid with saline hydration for cidofovir.
keyPoints: Up to one-third of patients receiving foscarnet develop nephrotoxicity; about one-fifth for cidofovir.
Administer IV saline before/after foscarnet doses and use probenecid with saline hydration for cidofovir to reduce nephrotoxicity.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · FOSCARNET | CIDOFOVIR

items.title: Resistance Patterns in Herpesvirus Infections
items.content: Herpesviruses can develop resistance to antiviral agents through mutations. Foscarnet and cidofovir have different resistance profiles, with some CMV strains resistant to both due to UL54 DNA polymerase mutations, while many foscarnet-resistant strains may remain sensitive to cidofovir.
keyPoints: Mutations in UL54 can cause resistance to foscarnet and cidofovir in CMV.
Many foscarnet-resistant HSV or VZV strains retain sensitivity to cidofovir.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · FOSCARNET | CIDOFOVIR

items.title: Letermovir for CMV Prophylaxis in Transplant Recipients
items.content: Letermovir, a dihydroquinazoline inhibitor of the CMV DNA terminase complex (UL51, UL59), is approved for prophylaxis against CMV infection and disease in adult CMV-seropositive recipients post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant or donor CMV-positive, recipient CMV-negative kidney transplant. It does not require dose adjustment if CrCl > 10 mL/min but must be decreased with cyclosporine use.
keyPoints: Inhibits CMV DNA terminase complex (UL51, UL59).
Approved for prophylaxis in specific transplant recipients.
No dose adjustment needed if CrCl > 10 mL/min.
Dose decrease required with cyclosporine.
Reduces clinically significant CMV infection by 38% compared to placebo over 14 weeks post-transplant.
Side effects: headache, nausea, diarrhea, peripheral edema.
No nephrotoxicity or myelosuppression.
Resistance more common in vitro than with ganciclovir/foscarnet; clinically significant resistance due to UL56 mutations reported.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · LETERMOVIR | MARIBAVIR

items.title: Maribavir for Refractory Posttransplant CMV Infection/Disease
items.content: Maribavir, a benzimidazole inhibitor of the CMV UL97 protein kinase, is approved for treating posttransplant CMV infection/disease refractory to ganciclovir, cidofovir, or foscarnet. It should not be used with ganciclovir due to antagonistic effects and can increase concentrations of tacrolimus, sirolimus, everolimus, and cyclosporine.
keyPoints: Inhibits CMV UL97 protein kinase and reduces viral egress from the nucleus.
Approved for refractory posttransplant CMV infection/disease treatment.
Antagonizes ganciclovir; should not be co-administered.
Resistance to maribavir also confers resistance to ganciclovir.
Increases concentrations of tacrolimus, sirolimus, everolimus, and cyclosporine.
Common side effect: taste disturbance.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · LETERMOVIR | MARIBAVIR

items.subtopic: TRIFLURIDINE AND VIDARABINE | INVESTIGATIONAL AND OTHER AGENTS
items.title: Mechanism of Action: Trifluridine and Vidarabine in Antiviral Therapy
items.content: Trifluridine is a thymidine analogue that incorporates into viral DNA, disrupting its synthesis. Vidarabine, an adenosine analogue, also integrates into viral DNA but specifically inhibits viral DNA polymerase. Both agents are used topically and have specific indications for acyclovir-resistant HSV infections.
keyPoints: Trifluridine is active against acyclovir-resistant HSV, CMV, and vaccinia virus.
Vidarabine approved for topical therapy of herpes keratitis and acyclovir-resistant mucosal HSV infections.
Both drugs are used topically only.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · TRIFLURIDINE AND VIDARABINE | INVESTIGATIONAL AND OTHER AGENTS

items.subtopic: TRIFLURIDINE AND VIDARABINE | INVESTIGATIONAL AND OTHER AGENTS
items.title: Clinical Use: Trifluridine and Vidarabine for Resistant Viral Infections
items.content: Trifluridine is indicated for acyclovir-resistant HSV, CMV, and vaccinia virus infections due to its mechanism of disrupting viral DNA synthesis. Vidarabine is used topically for herpes keratitis and resistant mucosal HSV infections by inhibiting viral DNA polymerase.
keyPoints: Trifluridine effective against acyclovir-resistant HSV, CMV, vaccinia virus.
Vidarabine approved for topical therapy of herpes keratitis and acyclovir-resistant mucosal HSV infections.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · TRIFLURIDINE AND VIDARABINE | INVESTIGATIONAL AND OTHER AGENTS

items.title: Timing and Efficacy of Neuraminidase Inhibitors for Influenza Treatment
items.content: Neuraminidase inhibitors, including oseltamivir, zanamivir, and peramivir, are most effective when initiated within 48 hours of symptom onset. Early treatment reduces symptoms by approximately one day in persons with uncomplicated influenza.
keyPoints: Oseltamivir is approved for treatment if started ≤ 48 hours after symptom onset.
Zanamivir and peramivir are effective when initiated within ≤ 2 days of symptoms.
Early treatment reduces symptoms by ~1 day in uncomplicated cases.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INFLUENZA (part 1)

items.title: Resistance Patterns and Considerations for Neuraminidase Inhibitors
items.content: Resistance to oseltamivir is more common among immunocompromised persons, with resistance rates of ~15% in healthy children and ~1% in adults. Zanamivir shows less resistance than oseltamivir and can be effective against some oseltamivir-resistant strains.
keyPoints: Oseltamivir resistance is reported in ~15% of healthy children and ~1% of adults.
Resistance is more common among immunocompromised persons.
Zanamivir has less resistance than oseltamivir and can treat some resistant strains.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INFLUENZA (part 1)

items.title: Prophylactic Use of Neuraminidase Inhibitors for Influenza
items.content: Oseltamivir and zanamivir are approved for prophylaxis in high-risk settings, with an efficacy estimated at ~70–90%. Prophylaxis should not be administered 2 days before or within 2 weeks after receiving a live attenuated influenza vaccine.
keyPoints: Oseltamivir and zanamivir are approved for prophylaxis in high-risk settings.
Prophylactic efficacy is estimated at ~70–90%.
Avoid administration 2 days before or within 2 weeks after live attenuated influenza vaccine.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INFLUENZA (part 1)

items.title: Baloxavir Use in Influenza Treatment and Prophylaxis
items.content: Baloxavir is an antiviral that inhibits the cap-dependent endonuclease of influenza viruses, approved for single-dose treatment and postexposure prophylaxis in individuals aged ≥12 years with symptoms ≤48 hours. It covers both A and B strains, including those resistant to neuraminidase inhibitors.
keyPoints: FDA-approved for single oral dose
Effective for influenza A and B, including resistant strains
Reduces symptoms by ~1 day
Not recommended within 2 days before or 2 weeks after live attenuated influenza vaccine
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INFLUENZA (part 2) | RESPIRATORY SYNCYTIAL VIRUS

items.title: Ribavirin in Severe RSV Infections
items.content: Aerosolized ribavirin is approved for treating severe lower respiratory tract infections due to RSV in hospitalized infants and young children, administered 12–18 hours per day. It reduces viral replication but has significant side effects and high costs.
keyPoints: Approved for aerosolized use in severe RSV
Administered 12–18 hours/day
Costs >$25,000 per day
Can cause bronchospasm and precipitate in ventilators
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INFLUENZA (part 2) | RESPIRATORY SYNCYTIAL VIRUS

items.title: Nirsevimab-alip for RSV Prevention
items.content: Nirsevimab-alip, a monoclonal antibody targeting the prefusion form of the RSV F protein, is approved to prevent lower respiratory tract disease due to RSV in neonates and infants up to 24 months old.
keyPoints: Approved for prevention of RSV in neonates and infants
Targets prefusion RSV F protein
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INFLUENZA (part 2) | RESPIRATORY SYNCYTIAL VIRUS

items.title: Remdesivir Use in Hospitalized SARS-CoV-2 Patients
items.content: Remdesivir is an antiviral drug approved for treating hospitalized patients aged ≥12 years with SARS-CoV-2, particularly those requiring supplemental oxygen. It shortens hospital stay duration by inhibiting viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.
keyPoints: Approved by FDA for persons ≥12 years needing hospitalization
Shortens hospitalization duration in lower respiratory tract disease
Recommended for patients requiring supplemental oxygen (NIH guidelines)
Not recommended if GFR <30 mL/min
Monitor liver enzymes due to potential serum transaminase elevations
Avoid concurrent use with chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · SARS-COV-2 (SEE CHAP.204)

items.title: Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir for High-Risk COVID-19 Patients
items.content: Nirmatrelvir, boosted with ritonavir, is approved for treating mild to moderate COVID-19 in adults at high risk of progression. It should be administered within 5 days of symptom onset and requires dose adjustments for renal impairment.
keyPoints: Approved for mild to moderate COVID-19 in high-risk adults
Administer as soon as possible after infection, within 5 days of symptoms
Avoid with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A due to severe reaction risk
Dose reduction necessary for renal impairment
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · SARS-COV-2 (SEE CHAP.204)

items.subtopic: INVESTIGATIONAL AGENTS FOR RESPIRATORY VIRUS INFECTIONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HUMAN PAPILLOMAVIRUS AND POXVIRUS INFECTIONS | INVESTIGATIONAL ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR PICORNAVIRUS
items.title: Favipiravir for Emerging Influenza Viruses
items.content: Favipiravir (T705) is an investigational antiviral agent that inhibits viral RNA polymerases, showing activity against influenza and other RNA viruses. It has received approval in Japan specifically for the treatment of emerging influenza viruses.
keyPoints: Inhibits viral RNA polymerases
Active against influenza and other RNA viruses
Approved in Japan for emerging influenza
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INVESTIGATIONAL AGENTS FOR RESPIRATORY VIRUS INFECTIONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HUMAN PAPILLOMAVIRUS AND POXVIRUS INFECTIONS | INVESTIGATIONAL ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR PICORNAVIRUS

items.subtopic: INVESTIGATIONAL AGENTS FOR RESPIRATORY VIRUS INFECTIONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HUMAN PAPILLOMAVIRUS AND POXVIRUS INFECTIONS | INVESTIGATIONAL ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR PICORNAVIRUS
items.title: Interferon-α for HPV Warts Treatment
items.content: Interferon-α (IFN-α) is an FDA-approved antiviral drug used intralesionally to treat external anogenital warts caused by human papillomavirus (HPV). It inhibits the replication of various RNA and DNA viruses in vitro.
keyPoints: Inhibits replication of many RNA and DNA viruses
FDA-approved for intralesional treatment of HPV warts
~50% resolution rate of lesions
~25% recurrence rate
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INVESTIGATIONAL AGENTS FOR RESPIRATORY VIRUS INFECTIONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HUMAN PAPILLOMAVIRUS AND POXVIRUS INFECTIONS | INVESTIGATIONAL ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR PICORNAVIRUS

items.subtopic: ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS B VIRUS INFECTION
items.title: First-Line Therapy for Chronic HBV Infection: Nucleos(t)ide Analogues
items.content: Nucleos(t)ide analogues are considered first-line therapy for chronic hepatitis B due to their antiviral potency, favorable side-effect profile, and ease of administration. They competitively inhibit HBV reverse transcriptase by acting as chain terminators of nascently replicating DNA.
keyPoints: Nucleos(t)ide analogues are first-line therapy for chronic HBV infection.
They act as chain terminators of nascently replicating DNA, inhibiting HBV reverse transcriptase.
Administered orally once daily.
Black box warning for lactic acidosis and severe hepatomegaly noted in older nucleoside analogues, not observed in newer agents.
Long-term therapy is necessary to prevent virus rebound and hepatitis flare (40–50% risk if stopped).
Viral resistance rates are lower with newer drugs like entecavir and tenofovir compared to older agents.
Histologic and biochemical improvement occurs in ~60–75% of patients without significant differences between antiviral agents.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS B VIRUS INFECTION

items.subtopic: ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS B VIRUS INFECTION
items.title: Pegylated IFN-α2a as First-Line Therapy for Chronic HBV Infection
items.content: Pegylated IFN-α2a is another first-line therapy option, offering advantages such as a finite dosing period and absence of viral resistance. However, it has lower rates of biochemical and virologic responses compared to nucleos(t)ide analogues.
keyPoints: Pegylated IFN-α2a is considered a first-line therapy for chronic HBV infection.
Advantages include finite dosing period (48 weeks), absence of viral resistance, higher rates of serologic response.
Lower rates of biochemical and virologic responses (<40% for both).
Poor tolerability due to numerous side effects; contraindicated in significant portal hypertension and pregnancy.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS B VIRUS INFECTION

items.subtopic: ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS B VIRUS INFECTION
items.title: Combination Therapy with Pegylated IFN-α2a and Nucleos(t)ide Analogues
items.content: Combining pegylated IFN-α2a with nucleos(t)ide therapy in treatment-naïve patients increases HBsAg loss rates compared to monotherapy. However, long-term nucleos(t)ide analogue monotherapy achieves similar outcomes with greater viral suppression.
keyPoints: Higher response rates with combination therapy of pegylated IFN-α2a and nucleos(t)ide analogues in treatment-naïve patients.
HBsAg loss after 48 weeks: 9.1% with combination, 0% with TDF alone (p<.001), 2.8% with IFN alone (p<.005).
Combination therapy not recommended due to similar HBsAg loss rates as long-term monotherapy and greater viral suppression.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS B VIRUS INFECTION

items.title: Resistance Profiles of Antiviral Agents in HBV Treatment
items.content: Understanding the resistance profiles is crucial for selecting appropriate antiviral therapy for chronic hepatitis B. Lamivudine, despite being a first-line agent initially, has high resistance rates over time, making it less favorable compared to newer agents.
keyPoints: Lamivudine resistance: ~30% after 1 year, ~70% after 5 years
Emtricitabine used in combination with tenofovir for HIV/HBV co-infection and selected cases of nucleoside resistance
Adefovir resistance develops in 20-30% of patients after 5 years
Telbivudine resistance: 25% in HBeAg-positive, 11% in HBeAg-negative after 2 years
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · LAMIVUDINE | ADEFOVIR | TELBIVUDINE

items.title: Clinical Use and Limitations of Telbivudine for HBV
items.content: Telbivudine is effective against HBV but has limitations due to resistance development, side effects, and economic factors leading to its market withdrawal in the U.S.
keyPoints: FDA approved in 2006 for chronic HBV treatment
Resistance rates: 25% in HBeAg-positive, 11% in HBeAg-negative after 2 years
Side effects include myopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and fatigue
Withdrawn from U.S. market primarily due to economic reasons
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · LAMIVUDINE | ADEFOVIR | TELBIVUDINE

items.title: Entecavir Dosing for Lamivudine-Resistant HBV
items.content: For patients with prior lamivudine exposure, a higher dose of entecavir (1 mg daily) is recommended due to the need for 20- to 30-fold higher concentrations to effectively inhibit some resistant HBV strains.
keyPoints: Entecavir blocks HBV polymerase, inhibiting priming and reverse transcription.
Higher dose of 1 mg/day recommended for patients with prior lamivudine exposure.
Resistance may occur in up to 50% of patients with prior lamivudine resistance after 5 years.
Entecavir-resistant strains remain susceptible to tenofovir.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ENTECAVIR | TENOFOVIR

items.title: Tenofovir Efficacy and Safety in HBV Treatment
items.content: Both TDF and TAF achieve high rates of viral suppression over 10 years, with TAF showing a higher biochemical response. Clinical resistance is rare up to 8 years, but renal toxicity risk is lower with TAF.
keyPoints: Tenofovir inhibits HBV replication effectively.
96–98% of patients achieve complete viral suppression after 10 years with TDF.
Clinical resistance not observed for up to 8 years.
Renal toxicity risk higher with TDF than TAF; no Fanconi syndrome reported with TAF.
Routine renal function monitoring required during therapy.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · ENTECAVIR | TENOFOVIR

items.subtopic: INTERFERONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS C INFECTION
items.title: Pegylated IFN-α2a in Chronic Hepatitis B: Efficacy and Predictors of Response
items.content: Pegylated IFN-α2a is used for treating chronic hepatitis B, offering a more convenient dosing schedule due to its pegylation. It results in the loss of HBV replication markers such as HBeAg and HBV DNA in 29–36% and 8–14% of cases respectively, with some patients also clearing HBsAg.
keyPoints: Pegylated IFN-α2a replaces standard IFN-α2b for chronic hepatitis B treatment.
Results in loss of HBeAg (29–36%) and HBV DNA (8–14%).
Predictors of favorable response include low pretherapy HBV DNA, high ALT levels, short duration of infection, genotypes A/B, and active liver inflammation.
Poor responses are seen in HBeAg-negative and immunosuppressed patients.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS C INFECTION

items.subtopic: INTERFERONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS C INFECTION
items.title: Goals and Outcomes of Hepatitis C Treatment with DAAs
items.content: The primary goal in treating hepatitis C is achieving a sustained virologic response (SVR), defined as undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks post-treatment, synonymous with cure. This outcome reduces mortality and hepatocellular carcinoma risk.
keyPoints: SVR achieved when HCV RNA remains undetectable 12 weeks after treatment ends.
Associated with reduced all-cause and liver-related mortality, and lower risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
Pangenotypic regimens like glecaprevir/pibrentasvir and sofosbuvir/velpatasvir have SVR rates >95% across HCV genotypes.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS C INFECTION

items.subtopic: INTERFERONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS C INFECTION
items.title: Managing HBV Reactivation Risk in HCV Treatment with DAAs
items.content: DAAs used for treating chronic HCV carry a black-box warning about potential HBV reactivation, particularly among HBsAg-positive individuals. This risk is rare and manageable through routine monitoring.
keyPoints: All DAAs have a black-box warning regarding HBV reactivation risk.
Risk is higher in HBsAg-positive patients and those with isolated anti-HBc.
Reactivation can lead to severe outcomes like fulminant hepatitis or hepatic flare.
Routine monitoring allows safe management of this risk.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | ANTIVIRAL DRUGS FOR HEPATITIS C INFECTION

items.title: Sofosbuvir Mechanism and Resistance Profile
items.content: Sofosbuvir is a prodrug that inhibits HCV NS5B RNA-dependent RNA polymerase, terminating viral RNA replication. It is approved for genotypes 1–4 but active against 1–6. Resistance due to S282T substitution in NS5B is rare and clinically significant resistance during treatment is exceedingly rare.
keyPoints: Sofosbuvir targets HCV NS5B polymerase
Active against genotypes 1-6, approved for 1-4
Resistance due to S282T substitution in NS5B is rare
Virologic breakthrough during treatment is exceedingly rare
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 1)

items.title: Sofosbuvir/Ledipasvir Dosing and Efficacy
items.content: The combination of sofosbuvir/ledipasvir is effective against genotypes 1, 4, 5, and 6 with SVR rates of 95–100%. Standard treatment duration is 12 weeks but can be reduced to 8 weeks for certain genotype 1-infected noncirrhotic patients. Treatment should be extended or ribavirin added in decompensated cirrhosis or previous DAA exposure.
keyPoints: Effective against genotypes 1, 4, 5, and 6
SVR rates of 95–100%
Standard treatment duration: 12 weeks
8-week regimen for certain genotype 1-infected noncirrhotic patients with baseline HCV RNA <6 million copies/mL
Extend to 24 weeks or add ribavirin in decompensated cirrhosis or previous DAA exposure
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 1)

items.title: Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir Spectrum and Tolerability
items.content: Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir treats genotypes 1–6 in naïve patients, including those with decompensated cirrhosis. SVR rates are 96–100% for non-cirrhotic patients across all HCV genotypes. The therapy duration is not shortened based on genotype or fibrosis stage. Velpatasvir should be taken with food and avoided with antacids or proton pump inhibitors.
keyPoints: Treats genotypes 1–6 in naïve patients
SVR rates of 96–100% for non-cirrhotic patients
No need to shorten therapy duration based on genotype or fibrosis stage
Take with food, avoid antacids/proton pump inhibitors
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 1)

items.subtopic: NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 2) | ☑ PROTEASE INHIBITOR-CONTAINING REGIMENS
items.title: Sofosbuvir/Daclatasvir: Treatment and Dosing Considerations in HCV Genotypes 1 and 3
items.content: The combination of sofosbuvir with daclatasvir is approved for treating HCV genotypes 1 and 3. Daclatasvir inhibits viral RNA replication and virion assembly by binding the N terminus of NS5A protein, administered alongside sofosbuvir for 12 weeks. It's safe for decompensated cirrhosis patients but requires dose adjustments with strong CYP3A inhibitors or moderate inducers.
keyPoints: Approved for HCV genotypes 1 and 3
Daclatasvir binds the N terminus of NS5A protein, inhibiting replication and assembly
Administered with sofosbuvir for 12 weeks
Safe for decompensated cirrhosis
Dose adjustments needed with strong CYP3A inhibitors or moderate inducers
Absorption not affected by food; highly protein bound
No dose adjustment required for renal impairment
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 2) | ☑ PROTEASE INHIBITOR-CONTAINING REGIMENS

items.subtopic: NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 2) | ☑ PROTEASE INHIBITOR-CONTAINING REGIMENS
items.title: Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir: Efficacy and Dosing in HCV Treatment
items.content: Glecaprevir, a pangenotypic NS3/NS4A protease inhibitor, is coformulated with pibrentasvir, an NS5A protein inhibitor. It's effective against HCV genotypes 1–6 with high resistance barriers. Treatment duration varies: 8 weeks for treatment-naïve patients and up to 16 weeks for those with prior NS5A exposure or cirrhosis. Not suitable for decompensated cirrhosis.
keyPoints: Effective against HCV genotypes 1–6
High genetic barrier to resistance
8-week course recommended for treatment-naïve patients
12 weeks for treatment-experienced cirrhotic patients
16 weeks with prior NS5A exposure
Taken with food; no dose adjustment in end-stage renal disease
Inhibits P glycoprotein, BCRP, and OATP1 transporters
Not appropriate for decompensated cirrhosis

items.subtopic: NS5B POLYMERASE AND NS5A-CONTAINING REGIMENS (part 2) | ☑ PROTEASE INHIBITOR-CONTAINING REGIMENS
items.title: Elbasvir/Grazoprevir: Considerations for HCV Genotypes 1 and 4 Treatment
items.content: Elbasvir/grazoprevir treats HCV genotypes 1 and 4, with reduced efficacy in genotype 1a if baseline RAVs are present. Baseline resistance testing is recommended for genotype 1a to determine the need for ribavirin addition and therapy extension. Treatment duration varies based on genotype and previous treatment exposure.
keyPoints: Active against HCV genotypes 1 and 4
Reduced efficacy in genotype 1a with baseline RAVs at positions M28, Q30, L31, Y93
Baseline resistance testing recommended for genotype 1a
Ribavirin addition and therapy extension if baseline RAVs are present
12 weeks treatment duration for genotype 1b or genotype 1a without baseline RAVs
16 weeks with ribavirin in patients with baseline NS5A polymorphisms or previous IFN exposure
No dose adjustment needed for chronic kidney disease or dialysis
Coadministration with moderate/strong CYP3A inducers or strong inhibitors not recommended

items.title: Role and Current Use of Interferons in HCV Treatment
items.content: Interferon therapy, once a cornerstone for chronic HCV treatment when combined with ribavirin, is no longer recommended due to the advent of direct-acting antiviral agents (DAAs) since 2014.
keyPoints: IFN/pegylated IFN with ribavirin was previously approved for chronic HCV infection.
Direct acting antiviral agents have replaced IFN therapy as first-line treatment.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | RIBAVIRIN

items.title: Mechanism and Role of Ribavirin in HCV Therapy
items.content: Ribavirin, a synthetic oral triazole guanosine analogue, weakly inhibits DNA and RNA polymerases. Its primary mechanism involves promoting viral replication errors and stimulating immune responses.
keyPoints: Ribavirin's role includes preventing virologic relapse during the IFN era.
Adverse effects include hemolytic anemia, rash, myalgia, and fatigue; it is teratogenic.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | RIBAVIRIN

items.title: Indications for Ribavirin Augmentation in DAA-Based HCV Therapy
items.content: Ribavirin augmentation enhances sustained virologic response (SVR) rates, particularly in genotype 1 infections and specific clinical scenarios.
keyPoints: Improves SVR rate by an average of 5% in treatment-naïve and experienced patients with genotype 1 infection.
Recommended for genotype 1a or 4 infection when combined with certain DAAs.
Useful in re-treatment of genotype 1-infected, therapy-experienced cirrhotic patients.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | RIBAVIRIN

items.title: Efficacy of IFN-α and Pegylated IFN-α in Hepatitis D Treatment
items.content: Interferon-alpha (IFN-α) and pegylated IFN-α are used off-label for treating hepatitis D, achieving a sustained virologic response (SVR) in 25–35% and 17–43% of patients, respectively, over 48 weeks. However, frequent relapse occurs post-treatment cessation.
keyPoints: IFN-α achieves SVR in 25–35% of hepatitis D patients over 48 weeks.
Pegylated IFN-α achieves SVR in 17–43% of hepatitis D patients over 48 weeks.
Virologic and biochemical relapse are common after stopping IFN treatment.
Extending treatment duration may maintain clinical response and HBsAg loss.
items.reference: Antiviral Chemotherapy Excluding Antiretroviral Drugs · INTERFERONS | ENTRY INHIBITORS

items.title: Bulevirtide as an Entry Inhibitor for Hepatitis D
items.content: Bulevirtide, approved in Europe at 2 mg subcutaneously daily, blocks hepatitis D virus (HDV) entry by binding to the hepatocyte receptor. It effectively suppresses HDV viremia and normalizes ALT levels in a significant proportion of patients.
keyPoints: Bulevirtide is approved in Europe for compensated chronic HDV infection at 2 mg subcutaneously daily.
Suppresses HDV viremia by at least 100-fold; normalizes ALT in 45% (2 mg) and 48% (10 mg) of patients.
Undetectable viremia achieved in 12.2% (2 mg) and 20% (10 mg) of patients.
Combined bulevirtide and pegylated interferon is more effective than bulevirtide alone for reducing HDV RNA to undetectable levels.
No viral resistance observed; common adverse reactions include elevated bile acids and injection-site reactions.

items.question: Why are drug-resistant RNA viruses more commonly found in healthy persons compared to DNA viruses?

Drug resistance is less common in immunocompromised patients.
RNA viruses have a higher mutation rate, leading to more frequent resistance.
DNA viruses are not affected by antiviral drugs.
items.explanation: RNA viruses have a higher mutation rate than DNA viruses, which leads to more frequent drug resistance even in healthy persons.

RNA viruses do not develop drug resistance.
Monotherapy is always sufficient for treating RNA viruses.
items.explanation: Combination therapy with antiviral agents can be more effective against RNA viruses because it targets multiple mechanisms of action, addressing different resistance patterns.

To maintain viral suppression and avoid hepatitis flare
To achieve higher rates of serologic response
To ensure complete clearance of HBsAg

Its long-term use is not limited by viral resistance.
Emtricitabine, when used alone, offers a significant advantage over lamivudine in treating HBV.
Lamivudine has been replaced as a treatment option due to its high activity against HIV.

Resistance to adefovir emerges faster than resistance to lamivudine.
It is effective for treatment-naïve patients and those with lamivudine-resistant HBV.
Adefovir is the first-line treatment for chronic hepatitis B due to its high barrier to resistance.
items.explanation: Adefovir dipivoxil is effective for managing HBV in treatment-naïve patients and those infected with lamivudine-resistant HBV, but it has been replaced by agents with higher barriers to resistance as first-line treatment.

Because of severe side effects including myopathy and peripheral neuropathy.

Entecavir resistance is common among previously untreated patients.
A higher dose of entecavir is recommended for patients with previous lamivudine exposure.

Renal toxicity is higher with TAF compared to TDF.
There have been no reported cases of Fanconi syndrome or proximal renal tubulopathy in clinical trials of TAF.
TDF has a lower risk of acute renal failure than TAF.

Dosing of entecavir does not need adjustment based on renal function.
Entecavir is cleared renally and requires dose adjustment if CrCl <50 mL/min.
